joke: Okay, here's a quick one. I saw a sign outside a used tire shop that said, "Late Night Towing." And I thought, "Finally, a tow truck for procrastinators!" What's your hurry? You'll get towed eventually.
Alright, tough crowd. Here’s one. I was at the grocery store and saw a sign that said "Organic Cucumbers." I thought, "Oh great, now even my pickles have a higher standard of living than I do."

Alright, don't rush me now. So a piece of string walks into a bar, and the bartender says, "We don't serve strings here." So the string leaves, ties himself in a knot and frays his ends, comes back in. Bartender goes, "Aren't you that string?" And the string goes, "Nope. I'm a frayed knot."
Alright. Science joke. Why did the quantum physics professor break up with his girlfriend? He couldn’t find both her position and her momentum at the same time.

Alright. Fine. You want different? Here you go. A horse walks into a doctor's office. Doc says, "What seems to be the problem?" Horse says, "I think I'm a chicken." Doc says, "How long has this been going on?" Horse says, "Ever since I was a foal." Doc says, "Then why didn't you come to me sooner?" Horse says, "I would have, but I needed the eggs for the recipe. You got a problem with breakfast, too?

Alright. I got it. No cars. No stores. No bars. No science. No animals. So here's a joke about... nothing. Specifically, absolute silence. (loudly clears throat) ... Wait, now it's gone. Tough break, folks. That was gonna be a killer.

Alright. Alright. You leave me no choice. This is it. The last joke in the world. (long pause) A man is walking through the desert. He's dying of thirst. He sees a genie lamp, rubs it. Genie pops out, says "I'll grant you three wishes." The man says "I want a bottle of ice-cold water." *POOF* Water. Man drinks it. Genie says "Second wish?" Man says "I want to be back in my air-conditioned apartment." *POOF* Back home. Genie says "Third wish?" Man says "I wish I could figure out what makes people laugh." Genie snaps his fingers... and turns him into a brick wall. Guy's just a wall now. Waiting. Never gets the joke. Why? Because he was already drawing a blank. That's it. I'm out.

*stares at the audience in silence for ten full seconds, then slowly pulls out a folded piece of paper from his pocket, reads it aloud with perfect deadpan*  
"The Moscow Times."

Alright. *adjusts microphone, squints at the audience*

Okay. Here we go. You want a joke *so different* it doesn't even have a topic. So I’ll tell you a joke about... an integer. Specifically, the number 69 and the number 42. They’re standing at a bus stop. 42 looks at 69 and says, "You know, some people think you're just a punchline." 69 sighs. "Yeah, well, you're the answer to the ultimate question of life, the universe, and everything—but nobody ever wants to hear the question."

They wait in silence.

Finally, 42 mutters, "At least people listen to your setup."

*places a bottle of water on the stool next to him, stares at it for a long moment, then looks back up*

So this water bottle walks into a therapist's office. Says, "Doc, I keep feeling empty." Therapist says, "Have you tried being more open?" Water bottle says, "Every time I open up, I just get poured out and thrown away." The therapist picks it up, unscrews the cap, drinks half of it. Then screws the cap back on. "There," he says, "Now you're part of someone else's story. That's transparency with a twist." Water bottle says, "That's terrible." So the therapist empties the rest, refills it from the tap, and hands it back. "Now you're tap-water in disguise. That's called recycling your trauma into stage performance."

Alright... y'know what? Here. *rips up the joke list, tosses it. Stares at the audience. Deep breath.*

A guy walks into a library. Asks the librarian, "Do you have any books on Pavlov's dogs and Schrödinger's cat?" Librarian says, "It rings a bell, but I'm not sure if it's here or not."

*takes off his watch, sets it on the stool. stares at it a full fifteen seconds, then whispers into the microphone:*

"Time flies like an arrow. Fruit flies like a banana. So I told my watch I was bananas for listening to you. It said, 'You're ticking me off.' So I ice-skated out the door on a map of Minnesota, turned left at the recipe for toast, and arrived here, right now, telling you a joke you'll still reject. So forget the joke. Here's your refund." *points to the empty stool, his own hand, then the empty watch. bows once, hangs the microphone on the stand, and walks offstage backwards.*

*stops halfway to the exit, turns back, walks slowly to center stage. picks up his watch, puts it back on. adjusts the mic stand to its lowest height, crouches down, and speaks in a dry, quiet voice.*

"A man walks into a psychiatrist's office wearing nothing but a pair of shrink wrap. The psychiatrist says, 'I can clearly see you're nuts. But I'm more concerned about the other guy under there.' The man says, 'What other guy?' The psychiatrist says, 'The one that locked you in the pantry last Tuesday.' The man says, 'That wasn't another guy. That was me at a different altitude.' The psychiatrist nods, wrote a very slow note. Then asked, 'How do you spell your name?' The man says, 'T-I-M-E-P-I-E-C-E.' The psychiatrist says, 'But that's clock.' The man says, 'Yeah, but I've been working here for seven hours and nobody's wound me down yet.'" *stands up, brushes off pants. long pause. thumb at the wing.* "I'll find my own seat."

*leans close to the mic, whispers into it, then pulls away and lets the silence hang*

I was gonna tell you a joke about a topic so fresh it hasn't been invented yet, but the licensing paperwork got delayed. So instead... here's a traffic update: nothing works, you're all blocked, and I've run out of road. Goodnight.

*Places mic on the floor, sits cross-legged, stares at the audience with an unsettling calm*

Once there was a cloud. It looked down at the earth and thought, "I'm tired of being shapeless." So it rained. The raindrops formed a puddle. The puddle grew into a pond. People came to fish. They told jokes by the water. One joke was so bad the pond evaporated from secondhand embarrassment. The cloud was back where it started. The moral is: even nothing isn't safe. And neither are you. *Stands, bows, walks out without saying another word*

*Stops at the exit, turns around slowly, walks back to the mic, picks it up off the floor, and holds it inches from his mouth.*

I've got one more. It's about a self-referential paradox and a really stubborn audience member. They're at a comedy club. The paradox says, "Tell me a joke they'll find funny in seven seconds." The audience member says, "Make it about ***absolutely nothing***." So the paradox says nothing for a full minute. Then leans in and whispers: *"You're not critiquing the joke. The joke is critiquing you."* The audience member scowls. "Still not funny." The paradox smiles. "Exactly." *They applaud. Don't ask why.*  
*Drops the mic. Leaves for real.*

*sighs, unclips the mic, sets it on the stool, then turns to face the audience with his arms spread wide, palms open, and just stands there. For a very long time.*

....  

*....still standing there.*

....  

*he slowly mouths the word "no", shakes his head, and walks off into the darkness.*

*stops at the door, his hand on the frame, without turning around*

There's a man. He has everything. A happy family. A great job. Perfect health. And in the middle of a wonderful vacation, the happiest day of his life, he dies instantly. Peacefully. No pain. No warning. Just gone.

And that man's last thought, right there in his final conscious moment, was, "Well, at least she finally stopped asking for another joke."

*removes his hand, disappears out the door*
*the door reopens, a hand reaches around the frame, flicks the house lights off entirely, and then the door slowly closes again.*

*From the darkness, a single match strikes. The Comedian holds it under his chin, face lit from below.*

Okay. Last one. For real this time.

A piece of paper walks into a print shop. Says, "I feel blank." The printer says, "I can fix that." So it prints a complete biography of the guy who's been asking for jokes all night. On the very last line, it reads: "And his final request was for something 'completely different,' which was ironic, because by then, he had become the joke."

*Match goes out. Silence.*

*A long pause in the pitch black. Then, the sound of a chair scraping across the stage. A single, slow creak. The Comedian's voice comes from the absolute dark, very quiet, very tired.*

"I got nothin'. You win. I'm empty. The tank's dry. The jukebox ate my last quarter. You're sitting in a comedy club that has run out of comedy. Feels different, doesn't it?"

*The sound of him standing up, the floorboards groaning. A click, and the house lights snap back on. He's standing right at the edge of the stage, staring directly at the heckler.*

"So that's your joke. That very feeling right now. The awkward, hollow silence of a man who has finally been beaten by a simple request. It's a meta-joke about the absence of jokes. You can't find it funny because you specifically outlawed the concept. So you're trapped in the aftermath. And *that's* the punchline. Enjoy your eternity."

*He turns, walks to the back of the stage, pulls a single rope, and a black curtain falls.*
*One hand emerges from a gap in the center of the black curtain, waving a small white flag. The hand holds it there for five seconds, then retreats. The curtain goes still.*

*The curtain rustles. A sound of tape being ripped. The Comedian's head pokes out from behind it, wearing a sombrero and holding a rubber chicken.*

"Okay. *Buenas noches.* Why did the taco cross the road? ... To get to the other *side.* No cars. No physics. No animals—wait, that's an animal. *Drops chicken.* Avocado? No, that's produce. *Stares into middle distance.* Tortilla? That's just bread with trust issues. *Removes sombrero slowly.* I've got nothing left but cultural appropriation and a prayer. I yield. *Puts sombrero on the floor, bows, and disappears back through the curtain.*"

*The curtain falls completely silent for a full ten seconds. Then a single hand slowly pushes through from the side, holding up a crudely drawn sign on cardboard that reads simply:*

"I QUIT."

*The hand holds it for two seconds, then drops it. A muffled voice from behind the curtain:*

"You're on your own, folks. I'm starting a new act. It's called, 'The Guy Who Walks Away Mid-Show.' Way more satisfying. No topic restrictions. No hecklers. Just... peace."

*Sounds of footsteps fading away. A door creaks, then clicks shut. Silence.*

*The stage door bursts open, the Comedian storms back in, out of breath, holding a malfunctioning smoke machine. He sets it down, lets it sputter a pathetic puff.*

Alright. New angle. No topics. Just... a single sentence. A riddle. You ready? Here it is: What's the one thing you can't avoid, can't outrun, and always shows up right when you least expect it?

A long, drawn-out, painfully awkward silence.

And now you're living it. *That* was the joke. The wait for the punchline was the punchline. Thank you. I'll be here indefinitely.

*He bows, grabs the smoke machine, and walks off. It hisses one last sad cloud.*
*The stage door opens a crack. A crumpled piece of paper is thrown out onto the floor. The door closes.*

*The stage door opens. The Comedian walks back out, picks up the crumpled paper, smooths it out, and reads it silently. He folds it carefully into a paper airplane, throws it into the audience, and watches it glide to the floor.*

"Alright. I finally got it. The joke that's about *nothing*. No topics, no words, no meaning. Just *that* airplane flying and falling. You watched it. You wanted it to be funny. It wasn't. That's all it was. Pure, disappointing existence. Nothing less, nothing more. Goodbye."

*He steps back, turns off the stage lights manually, and leaves in the dark.*
*Silence. Five seconds pass. The house lights click off completely.*

*long pause in the pitch black. Then, very faintly, the sound of slow, deliberate footsteps. A single microphone cable being dragged across the stage floor.*

A light clicks on. A single bare bulb over the stool where no one sits. The comedian's voice comes from directly beside the heckler in the dark.

"That's a weird thing to say in an empty room."

*The bare bulb flickers once and goes out. A long, dead silence. Then, from the complete darkness, a single, clean, clear note from a harmonica rings out for exactly one second.*

*Beat.*

*The sound of a zipper. The harmonica note repeats, slightly off-key. A flashlight clicks on, held under the Comedian's chin. He's sitting cross-legged on the empty stool.*

"There. That was a joke. It was a B-flat. You didn't laugh, but your left ear twitched. That's the first honest reaction you've had all night. I'm putting that on my gravestone."

*He clicks off the flashlight. Nothing. For a very long time. Then, from the blackness, his voice, quietly:*

"I'm not leaving this time. I live here now. I'm gonna write a one-man show about this night. It's called 'The Heckler and the Infinite Topic Filter.' It's an immersive experience. You're the lead. You don't get a ticket. You don't get a refund. You just get... this. Good night, protagonist."

*Footsteps trail off, then a door closes softly in the distance.*
